December 4,7991.
Martha F. Stanton
2091 East Valley Parkway
Escondido, C alifornia 92027
Re: Campaign Contributions
Dear Ms. Stanton:
I respond to your letter of November 26, 199L, in which you indicated some
continuing concern about the proper interpretation of Escondido Municipal
Code sections governing campaign contributions from a husband and wife.
The opinion memorandum issued by the office on April 14, 1986, is best
summarizeci as standing for the proposition that the Escondido Municipal Code
presumes that contributions from a husband and wife are considered to be from
a single source and are cumulative. However, as noted in the memorandum,
this conclusion only exists "absent unusual circumstances."
Indeed, this position has been expressed in previous advice to candidates, in
which this office advised that contributions from spouses will be added together
for the purpose of determining the $250.00 campaign contribution limit unless it
can be determined that "contributions come from separate as opposed to
community property." (Letter to Sid Hollins, dated lv{ay 8, 1,990, copy attached.)
In evaluating what standards are to be used in determining whether or not
campaign contributions are to be treated as from a single source, this office has
consistently looked to the definition found in the Political Reform Act of \974,
the Guidelines, and the decisions of the Fair Political Practices Commission (see
April 74,7986, memorandum, page2, second full paragraph.)
As you have noted in your November 26, 1,997,letter, the FPPC has specific
advice as to when contributions are to be considered from a single sourcl, and
when such contributions are to be considered from separate sources. The
C(


Martha F. Stanton
December 4, 1991
paBe2
position of the FPPC will apply in evaluations as to whether or not contributions
from a husband and wife come from a single or separate sources for purposes of
the Escondido Municipal Code campaign contribution limits.
I hope this answers your questions. If not please let me know.
|RE/jls

Jeffrey R. Epp

Regarding: CampaignContributions

Calilornia Soci€ty ol
Cerlifi 6d Public Accounlants
Dear Jeff:
Thank you for your patience in helping me to understand the ordinances governing the
campaign contributions. I have read the city Attorney's opinion dated April 14, 1986 and,
unfortunately, I am still confused. To quote:
"With respect to the question of contributions by spouses, the Fair Political
Practices Commission has specifically addressed the issue in informational
materials it distributes to candidates for office who are subject to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act. Consistent with the views expressed in
the Lumsdon and Kauhn opinions referenced above, the Commission has
concluded that absent unusual circumstances the 'contributions from a husband
and wife are considered to be from a single source and are cumulated...'
"Therefore, I conclude that in both of the circumstances posed in the questions
which you have presented the contributions must be added together and be
cons.idered as from a single source for the purpose of determining whether or not
the campaign contribution limits set forth in section 2-102 has been reached."
When I attended an FPPC workshop, we were specifically told that spouses could each
contribute the maximum from joint funds as long as both sign the check or there was an
accompanying document so instructing and signed by both parties. Also in looking in my
manual supplied by the FPPC, it is also very specific on this matter. I am enclosing a copy of
the page from the 1990 manual A. I do not have the l99l manual; but, FPPC informed me this
part has not changed.
Again, I apologize for being unable to grasp this concept. But as campaign treasurer for
Elmer Cameron, the campaign committee has requested clarification as we will begin collecting
contributions very soon. Unless we hear contrary from you, we will assume the city of
Escondido is following the current FPPC guidelines.
Sincerely,
Martha F. Stanton
Certified Public AccAuntant
Enclosure

For example, when a candidate is being voted upon jn a special election, (ontributors may contribute

if they contributed the maximum amount in the fis(al year to a committee established by the

NOTE: Transfers from a candidate's or officeholder's regular <ampaign bank account to a
campaign bank account established by the candidate or officeholder in connection with a special
elei:tion are permitted as long as such a transfer does not cause the total amount contributed by any
person to the special election account to ex(eed the contribution limitations. The same is true for
transfers from a special election campaign bank account to a regular <ampaign bank account.
D. AGGREGANON OF CONTRIBUTIONS
Contributions from an individual's personal funds and from any other funds which he or she in
fad dire(r and (ontrols must be aggregated.
Contributions made by two or more entities must be aggregated if the same person or a majority
of the same persons in fad directs and controls the contributions.

lndividuals may make separate contributions using jointly held funds (i.e., spouses may each
contribute 51,000 to a single candidate in a fiscal year). However, when a contribution is maje by a
check bearing the imprinted name of more than one individual, it must be attributed to the
individual who signs the check unless an accompanying document indi(ates the amount to be
attributed to each individual. lf each individual signs the (heck, the contribution must be attributed
equally to each individual unless an accompanying document indicates otherwise. A(companying
documents must be signed by each contributing individual.
